advantages: Shows all four error directions at once — correct positives/negatives and both mistake types (FP vs FN) — not collapsed into one accuracy number.
Concrete and interpretable for stakeholders: '80 false alarms and 12 missed cases' is actionable without knowing metric formulas.
Foundation for precision, recall, TPR, FPR, and accuracy — every common binary rate is a simple ratio from these four counts.
Supports error analysis — pull examples from FP or FN cells to improve labels, features, or threshold.
Extends naturally to multi-class problems as a K×K matrix revealing which class pairs confuse the model.
disadvantages: Tied to one classification threshold — changing the cutoff produces a different matrix; not threshold-independent like ROC-AUC.
High accuracy can hide bad performance on rare positives when negatives dominate — the matrix must be read row-by-row, not only via (TP+TN)/N.
Does not measure calibration — two models with identical TP/FP/TN/FN at 0.5 can have very different probability quality.
Layout convention matters — transposing actual/predicted axes or swapping positive class swaps FP/FN and breaks formula alignment.
Less informative alone for comparing models before the operating threshold is chosen — pair with ROC-AUC or PR-AUC during model selection.
